name: Francium
name_origin: Named after France, the home country of its discoverer Marguerite Perey.

occurrence.abundance_summary: Extremely rare; trace amounts produced in uranium and thorium ores from radioactive decay of actinium-227. Estimated less than 30 g exists in Earth's crust at any time.

compounds.name: Francium chloride
compounds.summary: Hypothetical alkali halide; expected to be highly water-soluble like other alkali chlorides, but only studied in trace ion-exchange experiments.

compounds.name: Francium perchlorate
compounds.summary: Used historically in coprecipitation studies with cesium perchlorate to confirm francium's chemical similarity to cesium.

uses: Atomic structure research, including measurements of fundamental symmetries and parity violation in heavy atoms
Studies of atomic energy levels in the heaviest naturally occurring alkali metal
reactivity_summary: Francium is the second most electropositive element and would be extremely reactive, igniting spontaneously in air and reacting violently with water. However, no observable bulk sample has ever been prepared because of its rapid radioactive decay.
isotopes_summary: All isotopes are radioactive; the longest-lived is francium-223 with a half-life of about 22 minutes, decaying by beta emission to radium-223. No stable isotopes exist.

citations.source: IUPAC Periodic Table of the Elements
CRC Handbook of Chemistry and Physics, 102nd ed.
